column3: According to the Greek text, Tobias came into captivity at age 37, lived in it 125 years, and died at age 158. He was blinded at age 58, lived blind for eight years, recovered his sight at age 66, and then lived another 92 years.

Tobias the younger, says Serarius, near the end of the book: when he was two years old, he was led into Assyria with his father. He went to Media, and there took Sara as wife, at age 23 or 24; he lived with his father still alive for 65 or 66 years; he lived after his father's death for 99 years; that is, in total 164 or 165 years. These last three figures are true according to the Greek text, which St. Ambrose follows, not the Latin Vulgate, which we must follow, as I shall presently say.

Furthermore, both Tobiases were led into Assyria in the sixth year of Hezekiah king of Judah; for in that year Shalmaneser, having conquered Samaria, carried the ten tribes away into Assyria, where on account of his virtue and uprightness Tobias was dear to king Shalmaneser; but sought out for death by his son Sennacherib after the defeat suffered in Judea while he was persecuting the Jews, he fled and hid himself, until after 45 days Sennacherib was slain by his sons, as is clear from ch. 1:24.

Since therefore Tobias the elder, in the sixth year of Hezekiah when he was led into Assyria, was 59 years old as stated, it necessarily follows that he was born in the 51st year of Uzziah king of Judah, when in the following year Isaiah ch. 6 saw that august vision of the Holy Trinity and of the Seraphim acclaiming him: Holy, Holy, Holy, Lord God of Sabaoth. Around the same time the Olympiads began, and Romulus and Remus, the founders of Rome, were born.

Tobias the elder died in the 40th year of Manasseh; for if from the 51st year of Uzziah you count 102 years (how long Tobias lived), you arrive at the 40th year of Manasseh. So say Salianus, Serarius, and Sanchez.

Tobias the younger was born in the 4th year of Hezekiah king of Judah, near the beginning of the city of Rome; and he died in the 16th year of Josiah king of Judah, when he was 99 years old, for that is the number of years the Vulgate edition assigns to him.

Tobias's genealogy is given in the Greek as follows: *The book of the words of Tobias, son of Tobiel, son of Ananiel, son of Adiel, son of Gabael, of the seed of Asiel* — the Hebrew adds *son of Nephthali*. Thus Tobias's father was Tobiel, his grandfather Ananiel, his great-grandfather Adiel (or perhaps Adiel), his great-great-grandfather Gabael, his great-great-great-grandfather Asiel, his great-great-great-great-grandfather Nephthali. His paternal grandmother was Debbora, for so it is read in the Hebrew and Greek at v.8: *As Debbora the mother of my father's brother commanded me, because I was left an orphan by my father.* This grandmother therefore reared Tobias well and piously, so that he owes his own uprightness to her. So Macrina, disciple of St. Gregory Thaumaturgus and grandmother of St. Basil, raising him together with his brothers and his sister Macrina in every virtue, formed him into a distinguished man and made him great. In like manner Ludmilla, nursing St. Wenceslas duke of Bohemia, rendered him both a saint and a martyr. # GEM

Furthermore, Tobias had a brother Anael, whose two sons are named in the Greek at v.8 as Achiacharus and Nasbas, or Nabac. Finally, from the phrase *the book of the words of Tobias* it is clear that Tobias is the author of this book, and accordingly in the Greek and Hebrew Tobias hereafter always speaks in the first person, saying: *I, Tobias, walked in the ways of truth*, etc. Again, it is evident from this that this book was written by the same author twice: first in Chaldean, but briefly, from which St. Jerome translated our Latin Vulgate; secondly also in Chaldean (though Serarius prefers Hebrew, and indeed twice — once by the father Tobias and a second time by his son), but more fully, from which the Greek version was translated — unless one prefers to hold that it was first written more fully, then somewhat abridged by the author himself or by someone else.

*From the tribe and city* [of Nephthali]: which city received the same name from the tribe, and therefore is called *properly* — as the Greek Complutensian has it (though the Roman edition has a word whose meaning is unknown) — that is, properly, Nephthali. From this tribe came many of Christ's disciples and Apostles, as is clear from Isaiah 9:1, of whom our Tobias was the type and perhaps ancestor.

*In the upper regions of Galilee*: from which, that is, the Jordan rising as from a higher place flows down to a lower, and therefore one Galilee is called upper and the other lower — just as Upper Germany is called that which lies alongside the Rhine at its source, flowing thence as from a higher to a lower place (which is accordingly called Lower Germany), as Serarius says. Upper Galilee is also called Galilee of the Gentiles, because part of it was given by Solomon to Hiram, the gentile king of Tyre, and was thereafter inhabited by Tyrians and other gentiles. The same region is also called Samaria, because the kings of Israel ruling from Samaria as their capital had Galilee subject to them; otherwise Samaria properly was not Galilee but lay between Galilee and Judaea.

Furthermore, this city of Nephthali, in which Tobias was born, is called Thesbe in the Greek — the same in which Elijah was born, whence he was called the Tishbite. So say Salianus, Serarius and others, though Adrichomius places Thesbe in the tribe of Gad, not Nephthali.

*When he was taken captive in the days of Salmanasar*: in Greek corruptly called Enemassar. Tobias was therefore taken captive in the sixth year of Hezekiah king of Judah, which was the ninth and last year of Hosea king of Israel, in which Salmanasar carried off the ten tribes, and among them Tobias, into Assyria (4 Kings 17–18). For although the father of Salmanasar, namely Tiglath-Pileser, had already carried off many from the ten tribes beforehand, he nonetheless likewise left many in Samaria and Nephthali, all of whom Salmanasar finally carried away.

*The way of truth* — that is, the way of the true faith and religion — *he did not forsake*. This is the chief praise of Tobias: that in captivity, surrounded by idolaters, he remained firm in the religion of his fathers. Captivity is a great temptation to apostasy, for captives, wishing to please their masters and to obtain liberty, readily conform themselves to their masters' customs and religion. But Tobias, mindful of God with his whole heart, persevered in God's ways and worship.

It is no small praise to have been good among the good; but it is praise of immense worth to have been good even among the wicked. St. Bernard, Sermon 48 on the Canticles: "It is no small title of perfect virtue," he says, "to live well among the wicked." # GEM

Among the wicked, to maintain innocence and purity of character. Examples are found in the Maccabees, Job, Lot (whom St. Peter praises for this reason in his Second Epistle, chapter two), in Noah and Abraham, and in

[IT HAPPENED THAT ALL THINGS WHICH HE COULD HAVE, HE DISTRIBUTED DAILY TO HIS FELLOW CAPTIVES AND BRETHREN (Israelites) WHO WERE OF HIS KINDRED,] both to console them in captivity, and to relieve their hunger and poverty, and to preserve them in true faith and religion, lest they should defect to the idols of the Assyrians in order to obtain from them the necessities of life. Tobias is here praised before his marriage for seven virtues: first, for constancy in faith; second, for charity toward his fellow citizens and tribesmen, v. 3; third, because he fled the company of the wicked, v. 5; fourth, for observance of the divine law, v. 6; fifth, for piety toward God, ibid.; sixth, because he faithfully offered to God all tithes and firstfruits, ibid.; seventh, because from boyhood he kept the other precepts of the law, v. 8.

[AND WHEN HE WAS THE YOUNGEST OF ALL, HE DID NOTHING CHILDISH IN HIS CONDUCT.] The same was done by St. Malachy, Bishop of Ireland; for as St. Bernard writes in his Life: He bore the manners of an old man while a boy in years, free from boyish wantonness. And Bernard adds one particular thing which shows that the boy gave a sign not merely of good but of great hope: for when he was drawn by the reputation of a certain master and came to him to learn letters, and saw him playing with a needle and making certain furrows in a wall, the grave boy, offended at the sight alone because it smelled of frivolity, withdrew from him. Such also does Gregory Nazianzen describe St. Basil in Oration 20. # GEM The golden maxim of St. Augustine on Psalm 112 is: Let your old age be childlike, and let your childhood be aged, that is, let your wisdom not be with pride, nor your humility without wisdom, so that you may praise the Lord henceforth, now and forevermore. For as Seneca complains of some in Epistle 4: We have the authority of old men and the vices of boys — not even of boys only, but of infants. Those are fickle, these have false fears, we have both.

[WHEN ALL WENT TO THE GOLDEN CALVES (set up and exalted as gods by Jeroboam the first king of Israel, 3 Kings 12:28), HE ALONE FLED THE COMPANY OF ALL, AND PROCEEDED TO JERUSALEM,] to worship the true God in the temple. Alone — that is, nearly alone, for the Hebrew and Greek add that Ananias and Jonathan went with him. Tobias therefore was like a sun of Israel; for the sun is so called because it alone gives light. St. Epiphanius in Heresy 30 praises Joseph, who was made a companion by Constantine the Great, because in Scythopolis he alone was orthodox while all the rest were Arians. St. Gregory Nazianzen in Oration 20 praises St. Basil because he avoided with him the corrupt morals of the Athenian Academy. If any river, he says, is believed to flow sweet through the salt sea, if in the fire which consumes all things some animal lives safely, such were we amid all who were growing depraved and corrupt. And since the laughter and taunts of many must be endured, let Epictetus be heard in the Enchiridion.
Concerning the Maccabees: 2 Macc. 6 and 7, who preferred to be tortured by Antiochus and fried in a frying pan rather than eat pork forbidden by the Law.
And because he was mindful of the Lord (and all His precepts) with his whole heart — that he might apply his whole heart and give it to knowing and carrying out in all things the law and will of God.
God repaid him in kind and with grace; for, as follows: GOD GAVE HIM GRACE IN THE SIGHT OF SALMANASAR THE KING. From this royal favor he received three notable benefits from him.

The first is expressed in v. 14: And he gave him, it says, power to go wherever he wished, having liberty to do whatever he could; and Tobias made excellent use of this liberty by visiting the houses of the Hebrews and giving them saving counsel, as follows.

The second benefit was ample gifts and presents, so great that from them he entrusted ten talents to Gabael, and made copious alms to needy Hebrews, as is clear from vv. 19 and 20. Tobias was therefore rich and wealthy from royal gifts and office.

The third benefit is added by the Greek, which has: And I was the King's buyer, Greek κυριακός, that is, buyer and agent for all things pertaining to the king, whether provisions, clothing, horses, arms, estates, etc.; Tobias was therefore steward of the royal household, as Joseph was to Pharaoh, Gen. 45; whence the Hebrew has: He was set over all that Salmanasar had. Tobias was therefore the king's oeconomus, which is the first dignity in the royal court, and therefore honored and of great salary and profit; from which Tobias became wealthy enough to assist all needy Hebrews with his liberality.

AND FROM THOSE THINGS WITH WHICH HE HAD BEEN HONORED (that is, gifted and enriched) BY THE KING, HE HAD TEN TALENTS OF SILVER. Some, says Serarius, think these ten talents amounted to 3,072 French crowns; others six thousand, because they assign six hundred crowns to one talent; others, like Cardanus in his book de Libris suis, assign five hundred ducats to one talent, so that ten talents would make five thousand ducats. Nothing certain can be assigned here, since we do not know whether this talent was Babylonian, Persian, or Hebrew; but it is certain it was an enormous sum of many thousands of gold pieces; whence the wealth of Tobias is apparent, as equally his charity and liberality toward Gabael and other poor Hebrews.

AND WHEN HE SAW GABAEL IN NEED, OF MUCH MULTITUDE OF HIS OWN KINDRED (his people), WHO WAS OF HIS TRIBE (Naphtali), UNDER A BOND (prudently, that he might be secured regarding the debt), HE GAVE HIM THE AFORESAID WEIGHT OF SILVER. For "gave," the Greek has προθέμενος, that is, "deposited," as if this money were a deposit given to Gabael to keep until Tobias should reclaim it and distribute it among the needy Jews living in Rages and Media; yet in such a way that he gave Gabael the power to use it for his urgent necessity; wherefore this deposit was at the same time a loan; but Tobias exacted no usury on the loan, indeed he asked for nothing even for profit foregone and damages incurred, says St. Ambrose, lib. de Tobia, c. 24: He sought no pledge, he demanded no surety. # GEM

And cap. 2: Consulting honor more than necessity, he lent his resources to his neighbor who lacked them, and did not demand them back throughout his whole life in such great need. When he saw himself worn out, as if a deposit entrusted to old age, he informed his son: not so much desiring to reclaim what was lent, as anxious not to defraud his heir. See here the charity and liberality of Tobias. # GEM

AFTER MUCH TIME, SALMANASAR DYING, WHEN SENNACHERIB HIS SON REIGNED IN HIS PLACE, AND HATED THE CHILDREN OF ISRAEL, from the beginning of his reign, but especially after receiving the defeat of 185,000 slain by the Angel in Judaea, in the year of Hezekiah king of Judah.
TOBIAS RAN DAILY THROUGH ALL HIS KINDRED, AND COMFORTED THEM, AND DISTRIBUTED TO EACH ONE AS HE WAS ABLE FROM HIS MEANS.

HE FED THE HUNGRY, AND PROVIDED CLOTHING FOR THE NAKED, AND WAS SOLICITOUS TO BURY THE SLAIN. Behold, these are the works of piety and mercy, both corporal and spiritual, in which Tobias piously and holily exhausted his wealth and strength, on account of which he obtained the hope, palm, and crown of martyrdom; just as St. Januarius, St. Marius and Martha with their sons, and many others who visited, fed, and buried the bodies of martyrs obtained the crown of martyrdom. An imitator of Tobias was St. Malachy Bishop of Ireland, of whom St. Bernard writes thus in his Life near the beginning: His greatest care was in burying deceased poor people, which surpassed his humility no less than his humanity. Nor was there lacking for our new Tobias a temptation revived from a woman, indeed from the serpent through a woman. His sister-in-law, shuddering at the indignity (as it seemed to her) of the office, said: What are you doing, madman? Leave the dead to bury their own dead. And reproaching him with this daily she pressed it upon him, but the foolish woman was answered according to her folly. Wretch, you hold the words of chaste speech, but ignore the virtue. So he, devoted, held the ministry to which he had come under compulsion, and exercised it without weariness. # GEM

BUT WHEN IT WAS REPORTED TO THE KING (SENNACHERIB), HE ORDERED HIM TO BE KILLED, AND TOOK ALL HIS SUBSTANCE. Tobias therefore willingly accepted the plundering of all his goods, flight, exile, and indeed a sentence of death, for his faith and charity; he was therefore himself a confessor and by vow a martyr. Hear St. Ambrose, lib. de Tobia, c. 1: On account of the virtues he was endowed with and practiced, he underwent the hardship of captivity, which he bore humbly and patiently, regarding it more as a common injury than a private misfortune. # GEM

[On Tobias fleeing and his virtues] He lamented not that the merits of virtue profited him nothing, but rather judged that contumely to be a lesser price for his sins. Piously and wisely says St. Bernard in his Sentences: "Martyrdom without blood is threefold: frugality in abundance, which David and Job possessed; generosity in poverty, which Tobias practiced; chastity in youth, which Joseph used in Egypt; and widowhood in the Gospel."
TOBIAS TRULY WITH HIS SON AND WITH HIS WIFE FLED AND HID NAKED.] The Hebrew states that Tobias hid for nineteen days.
AFTER CERTAIN DAYS (from the disaster received, and flight from Judah) HIS SONS KILLED THE KING.] See what is said at 2 Kings 19:37.

AND TOBIAS RETURNED TO HIS HOUSE AND ALL HIS SUBSTANCE (of wealth) WAS RESTORED TO HIM.] Namely, whatever still remained. For it is clear from the fact that Anna, the wife of Tobias, supported herself by weaving, that much had been consumed, plundered, sold off, and dissipated by the royal officials, attendants, and soldiers.

After these things, when a feast day of the Lord had come and a good meal had been prepared in the house of Tobias, he said to his son: Go and bring yet some more from our tribe who fear God, that they may feast with us.

When his son had gone and returned, he reported to him that one of the sons of Israel lay slain in the street. Immediately leaping from his place at table, leaving the meal, he went fasting to the body.
Taking it up, he carried it to his own house secretly, so that when the sun had set he might bury him with due care.
When he had hidden the body, he ate bread with mourning and trembling.
Remembering that word which the Lord spoke through the prophet Amos: Your feast days shall be turned into lamentation and mourning.

SYNOPSIS OF THE CHAPTER: Tobias, wearied from the burial and falling asleep, is blinded by the dung of swallows; whereupon, afflicted with reproaches by his wife and friends, he endures all things with wonderful patience, like another Job.

[On the feast day]: The Vulgate adds 'of the Lord' — that is, one of the three solemn feasts prescribed by the Law, namely Passover, Pentecost, or Tabernacles, in which all males were commanded to appear before the Lord. Tobias, though in exile, observed these as best he could.

[Go and bring some from our tribe]: Tobias, even in captivity, was zealous for the works of mercy and piety — not only burying the dead, but also gathering the poor and God-fearing to his table. He thus fulfilled both corporal works of mercy simultaneously.
[He went fasting]: Note the fervent charity of Tobias — he left his own meal and went fasting to care for the dead. Fasting joined with works of mercy is most acceptable to God. # GEM
[He carried it to his own house secretly]: He acted with prudence, hiding the body lest the king's officers discover it, for Sennacherib had forbidden the burial of those he had slain.

[He ate bread with mourning and trembling]: Tobias did not neglect bodily necessity, but tempered it with grief for the afflictions of his people. He ate not for pleasure but for sustenance, his soul occupied with sorrow.

[The word spoken through Amos]: See Amos 8:10 — 'I will turn your feasts into mourning.' Tobias recalled this prophecy, understanding that the calamities of Israel were the fulfillment of divine judgment long foretold. Thus he grieved not with mere human sorrow but with a holy, prophetically-informed grief.

WHEN IT WAS A FEAST DAY] Of Pentecost, as the Hebrew and Greek versions have it. For on this day especially one ought to be engaged in works of charity. For these are works of the Holy Spirit, whose feast is celebrated at Pentecost, Acts 2:1 and following.

AND WHEN THE SUN HAD SET, he buried him secretly, lest anyone should accuse him before the king and he should face danger to his life, as he had done in chapter 1:22. BURIED HIM.] St. Ambrose says in chapter 1: he was not restrained by the prohibition, but rather spurred on, lest he seem to abandon the duty of piety out of fear of death: the reward of mercy was the penalty of death. The same author, in book 1 of his Offices, chapter 4, says: Tobias expressed the form of piety more clearly when, leaving the feast, he buried the dead, and invited the poor to the food of a poor man's table. And St. Augustine, in his book On the Care for the Dead, chapters 1 and following, says: Tobias is commended for having merited God's favor by burying the dead, as the Angel testifies. For burial is the last office of mercy, a debt owed to nature, as it were an honor for the deceased and a consolation for relatives, and the desire of nature. Hear St. Ambrose.

BUT ALL HIS NEIGHBORS REBUKED HIM, SAYING: Already for this cause thou wast commanded to be put to death, and thou didst scarcely escape the sentence of death, and dost thou again bury the dead?] # GEM St. Ambrose, book 1 of Offices, chapter 4, commenting on this passage: The neighbors reproached Tobias, but he, unmoved, persevered in piety. For it is characteristic of a great soul not to be deterred by the contempt of others from performing works of virtue and mercy. Indeed, reproach and mockery from the wicked are frequently the marks and testimonies of virtue.

BUT TOBIAS, FEARING GOD MORE THAN THE KING.] This is the mark of a true servant of God, who prefers the fear of God to all human fears and dangers. For, as St. Chrysostom says, whoever truly fears God fears nothing else. He therefore carried off the bodies of those who had been slain and hid them in his house, and buried them in the middle of the night, at great personal risk.

IT CAME TO PASS THAT ON A CERTAIN DAY, BEING WEARIED WITH BURYING, HE CAME TO HIS HOUSE AND CAST HIMSELF DOWN BY A WALL AND SLEPT.] Note the heroic virtue and tireless charity of Tobias: even when exhausted by the laborious work of burying the dead, he did not cease from this pious duty. This is a model for all who perform works of mercy: they should not desist on account of weariness or difficulty.

AND HOT DUNG FROM A SWALLOWS' NEST FELL UPON HIS EYES WHILE HE SLEPT, AND HE BECAME BLIND.] This blindness came upon him not as a punishment for sin, but as a trial of virtue, as the next verse makes clear. Just as Job was afflicted not for his sins but to prove his virtue, so Tobias was permitted to be blinded in order that his patience might be manifested and praised.

NOW THIS TRIAL THE LORD THEREFORE PERMITTED TO HAPPEN TO HIM, THAT AN EXAMPLE MIGHT BE GIVEN TO POSTERITY OF HIS PATIENCE, AS ALSO OF HOLY JOB.] # GEM Here the inspired author himself explains the cause of Tobias's blindness: it was permitted by God not as punishment but as a trial and example. St. Ambrose, in his work on Tobias, draws the comparison with Job at length, showing that both men were afflicted by God's permission in order to manifest their virtue to all ages. The afflictions of the just are not signs of divine wrath but of divine love and confidence in their virtue.

FOR AS FROM HIS INFANCY HE HAD ALWAYS FEARED GOD AND KEPT HIS COMMANDMENTS, HE WAS NOT TROUBLED AGAINST GOD THAT THE PLAGUE OF BLINDNESS HAD BEFALLEN HIM.] Here the piety of Tobias from his very infancy is noted. He had from childhood feared God and kept His commandments. This early formation in piety was the foundation of his steadfastness in suffering. Those who are trained in virtue from youth bear adversity with far greater equanimity than those who come late to virtue.

BUT HE REMAINED IMMOVABLE IN THE FEAR OF GOD, GIVING THANKS TO GOD ALL THE DAYS OF HIS LIFE.] # GEM This is the summit of patience and virtue: not merely to endure suffering without murmuring, but to give thanks to God in the midst of affliction. St. John Chrysostom, in his homilies on patience, especially commends this practice of giving thanks in adversity, showing that it is more meritorious than bearing suffering in mere silence.

FOR AS KINGS INSULTED HOLY JOB, SO HIS RELATIVES AND KINSMEN MOCKED HIS LIFE.] The comparison with Job is again made explicit. As Job's friends, who should have consoled him, instead reproached and accused him, so the relatives of Tobias, who should have supported him in his blindness and poverty, instead mocked his piety and the hope he placed in God. This is a common trial of the just: those nearest to them often prove their greatest tormentors.

WHERE IS THY HOPE, FOR WHOSE SAKE THOU GAVEST ALMS AND BURIEDST THE DEAD?] This taunt of the relatives contains an implicit denial of Providence and divine reward. They argue, in effect, that Tobias's piety has brought him only misfortune, and therefore God either does not exist or does not care for those who serve Him. This is precisely the argument of the wicked in all ages against the suffering of the just.

BUT TOBIAS REBUKED THEM, SAYING: SPEAK NOT SO.] Tobias does not merely suffer in silence but actively refutes the impious reasoning of his mockers. This is the duty of the just man: not only to endure unjust reproach with patience, but to correct erroneous and impious speech when the honor of God demands it.

FOR WE ARE THE CHILDREN OF SAINTS, AND LOOK FOR THAT LIFE WHICH GOD WILL GIVE TO THOSE WHO NEVER CHANGE THEIR FAITH FROM HIM.] # GEM This verse contains a profound expression of faith in eternal life and in the covenant relationship between God and His faithful servants. The phrase "children of saints" refers to the patriarchs and holy ancestors of Israel, whose faith and example are an inheritance and an obligation. The hope of eternal life is here set forth as the true foundation of patience in suffering and perseverance in virtue. St. Ambrose frequently cites this verse as a model of Christian hope.

BUT ANNA HIS WIFE WENT TO WEAVING WORK DAILY, AND SHE BROUGHT HOME WHAT SHE COULD GET FOR THEIR LIVING BY THE LABOR OF HER HANDS.] The humble and industrious charity of Anna is here commended. Though it was humbling for a woman of her position to work as a weaver for hire, she did so cheerfully to support her blind husband. This is a model of conjugal devotion and industrious virtue.

WHEREBY IT CAME TO PASS THAT SHE RECEIVED A YOUNG KID OF THE GOATS AND BROUGHT IT HOME.] This kid, given to Anna perhaps as a bonus or gift beyond her wages, becomes the occasion for the display of Tobias's scrupulous justice and also of Anna's momentary impatience with his virtue.

AND WHEN HER HUSBAND HEARD IT BLEATING, HE SAID: SEE THAT IT BE NOT STOLEN; RESTORE IT TO ITS OWNERS, FOR IT IS NOT LAWFUL FOR US EITHER TO EAT OR TO TOUCH ANYTHING THAT COMETH BY THEFT.] The scrupulous justice of Tobias is here admirably displayed. Even in his blindness and poverty, even when a good meal was within reach, he refused to profit from anything that might have been obtained unjustly. This is a model of integrity: the just man maintains his principles not only when it is easy but especially when it is costly.

BUT HIS WIFE BEING ANGRY ANSWERED: IT IS EVIDENT THY HOPE IS COME TO NOTHING, AND THY ALMS ARE LOST.] Anna's angry response, though humanly understandable, represents a momentary failure of faith. She uses the occasion of the kid to renew the reproach she had heard from the neighbors: that Tobias's piety had brought him no reward. This is a common temptation for the spouses of saintly persons: to resent the hardships that devotion to virtue brings upon the family.

AND WITH THESE AND OTHER SUCH WORDS SHE UPBRAIDED HIM.] The patience of Tobias is further tried by the reproaches of his own wife, whom he loved. This domestic trial, added to blindness and poverty, constitutes a full participation in the sufferings of Job. The commentary of St. Gregory the Great on Job may profitably be applied here: the just man is tried in every relationship and every possession until only God remains as his support and consolation.

It happened one day (Greek: "that same night which followed the day of Pentecost mentioned in v. 1") that he cast himself down beside a wall. The Hebrew adds: "And I did not immediately enter my house, because I was unclean on account of the dead" (from contact with the corpse he had buried, according to the law of Num. 19:11ff.), "but I slept with open eyes outside the courtyard, beside the wall under the open sky, where swallows nest and discharge their droppings to the ground, fouling everything."

And from the swallows' nest warm droppings fell upon his eyes as he slept, and he became blind. — "Swallows": the Greek has strouthia, that is, sparrows; but the Septuagint renders the Hebrew צִפֳּרִים (sipporim) as strouthia, meaning "birds" in general, as Vatablus translates. From the circumstances of the place, our author [the Vulgate translator] rightly understood these birds to be swallows; for the dung of swallows blinds the eyes. Moreover, a second Hebrew text has here דְּרוֹר (deror), that is, a free bird, namely the swallow, which flies freely, lives freely among men, and nests in rooftops, as Rabbi David says. Hence the Pythagorean symbol: "Do not keep swallows under the same roof" — that is, beware of association with the garrulous and whispering — as St. Jerome explains in his work against Rufinus on the Apocalypse, and Clement of Alexandria, Stromata, bk. 5, ch. 3. So also among the Latins any bird whatsoever is sometimes called a sparrow (passer), as Serarius shows here.

And he became blind — because the dung of swallows is dry, hot, and caustic, and therefore dries out the moisture of the eyes, and so blinds naturally. So says Francis Valesius, Sacred Philosophy, ch. 62. Lyra therefore is less correct in denying that swallow dung has this blinding power. More probably, Dionysius and Valesius judge that Tobit was not truly blinded, but that only a film and membrane were drawn over his eyes — which surgeons, removing it with a needle, restore to full freedom and sight; more on this in chs. 6 and 9. Furthermore, for this reason the chicks of swallows, wallowing among their parents' droppings, are blinded, but their sight is restored by their parents through the herb called celandine (chelidonia). Hear Tertullian, On Penitence, ch. 12: "The swallow, if she blinds her chicks, knows how to restore their sight again with her own celandine." And Pliny, bk. 8, ch. 27: "Swallows have shown that celandine is most beneficial to sight, using it to heal the injured eyes of their young."

We are commanded to clothe the naked: how much more ought we to cover the bodies of the dead? If we are accustomed to escort travellers on a long journey, how much more those who have set out for that eternal home from which they shall not return? Nothing is more excellent than this service: to give to one who can no longer repay you; to rescue from birds, to rescue from beasts, one who shares your nature. Even wild beasts are said to have shown this humanity to dead bodies — shall men refuse it? # GEM

Add that the bodies of the faithful and the saints must be buried, so that by this we may profess our faith and hope of resurrection — namely, that they are not cast away like the carcasses of horses and dogs, whose souls perish with the body, but are buried as destined to rise again in soul and body to immortal life. Again, through burial great honor is rendered to the relics of saints and martyrs; hence the great care of the early Pontiffs to bury the martyrs. Indeed, St. Nicomedes by burying the body of St. Petronilla the Martyr, and St. Justin the priest by burying St. Laurence, and many others, attained the crown of martyrdom through the burial of martyrs.

So Marcianus, when he later came upon the freshly slain body of a man and wished to bury it, was seized as though he himself had killed him, and was condemned to death; but by God's providence the true author of the murder was discovered, and Marcianus was set free — so says Evagrius, bk. 2, ch. 1. By this work Marcianus merited to be raised to imperial power and to become the husband of St. Pulcheria, sister of the Emperor Theodosius — than whom no emperor was better, braver, or more pious: so Baronius, year of Christ 450.

The eyes of young swallows, if irritated by a thorn, heal again and afterward recover the power of sight completely. Now, says Aldrovandus in his work on the Swallow, p. 663, it is one thing to recover impaired sight, quite another for plucked-out eyes to grow back again, which is what Aelian and Pliny assert. The same Aldrovandus, p. 689, affirms from Dioscorides and Avicenna that the ash of a young swallow sprinkled with honey upon the eyes dispels their cloudiness by bringing clarity; indeed he contends from Albertus Magnus that the bile and cold dung of swallows (for warm dung is sharp and burning, whence it blinded Tobias) avails for the same purpose.

#GEM Tropologically: Blessed Peter Damian, book 2 of his Letters, letter 12 (letter 77 in order), says: "What do the lightly flitting swallows signify but the light character of flatterers and smooth-speakers, who, while they soothe with the sweetness of their honeyed speech, while they anoint the head of the listener with the oil of flattery, blind his interior eyes lest they enjoy the light of truth unimpeded?"

Again Dionysius the Carthusian says: "We see that droppings sometimes fall from the nests of heretical swallows upon the sleeping Church, and many are blinded." Furthermore, Thomas of Cantimpré, bk. 1, ch. 56, explains how swallows and other birds are hostile to bees — that is, how demons are hostile to pious and chaste souls. He says: "It can also signify another kind of temptation — the harassment of swallows (with whose dung Tobias was blinded) or of other birds — so that under the guise of virtues we are wrapped in the darkness of vices. For example: someone seeks to become rich and to amass wealth, so that in the end he may draw up a more generous will and make Christ his heir — yet he abandons the management of his resources to the lords of the land, or to his parents, or to his dependents, or to whatever most wicked plundering possessors there may be. We see this especially among clerics, whose goods ought to belong to the poor, and whatever is superfluous to them each day ought not to be stored in purse or chest, but ought to be liberally distributed to the needy mouth of the hungry and into the bellies of the starving."
THE LORD PERMITTED THIS TO HAPPEN TO HIM SO THAT AN EXAMPLE OF HIS PATIENCE MIGHT BE GIVEN TO POSTERITY, AS ALSO WITH HOLY JOB. Tobias therefore and Job are two examples of patience and...

This is a mirror for all the blind and afflicted, to be constantly gazed upon and imitated; for nothing evil befalls any faithful person except by the certain foreknowledge and will of God, either to purge him from vices, or to perfect him in virtue and patience, and thus to increase his merits and rewards in heaven. Thus God permitted the just Abel to be slain by his brother's impiety; Abraham to be tested by the holocaust of his dearest son; Joseph to be wickedly sold by his brothers; Moses with the entire Israelite people to be oppressed by the cruel tyranny of Pharaoh; David to be subjected to the implacable hatred of King Saul; Susanna to be exposed to the calumnies of the impure; the Prophet Isaiah to be sawn asunder with a saw; Daniel to be thrown to the lions. See St. Chrysostom, Homily 1 to the people, enumerating ten reasons why God permits the saints to be afflicted. Truly St. Augustine, Sermon 226 de Tempore, which is on Tobias, says: "To this end he incurred the exile of blindness, that he might receive an Angel as his physician."

NOT GRIEVED AGAINST GOD] So as to murmur or complain and say: "Lord, why did you permit me, in the course of your work—namely the burial of the dead—to be defiled by a swallow and blinded?" Hear St. Ambrose, book de Tobia, c. 2: "The prophet, wearied by so holy a duty, while resting beside the wall of the court, was struck by dung falling from a sparrow's nest and fell into blindness with a white film, yet he neither complained nor groaned, nor said: 'Is this the reward of my labors?' He grieved more that he was deprived of performing services than of the gift of his eyes; nor did he consider blindness a punishment, but an impediment."

Moreover, the Greek text and from it St. Ambrose add: "And white films formed over my eyes. And I went to physicians, and they did not help me; but Achiacharus (my nephew from my brother) nourished me until I departed to Elymais, to avoid danger"—of which more at the end of the last chapter. For it seems that Tobias, after his blindness, was again accused before the king by someone and despoiled of his goods, but, as the Greek and Hebrew have it, was fed and sustained by Achiacharus. But Achiacharus himself appears for this reason to have suffered calumny, as is stated in the Greek of the last chapter, and then Tobias either was sent into exile or fled to Elymais, as we read in the Greek. So Serarius and others.

BUT HE REMAINED UNMOVED IN THE FEAR OF GOD, GIVING THANKS TO GOD ALL THE DAYS OF HIS LIFE] This is an act of heroic patience; this is the state of a holy and perfect man, who, despising all earthly things, both adverse and prosperous, dwells in mind in heaven, and already begins to foretaste celestial happiness. Thus Job, afflicted on all sides, said in c. 1:21: "The Lord gave, the Lord has taken away; blessed be the name of the Lord." In all adversities therefore give thanks to God, and you will be holy, indeed blessed. Furthermore the Author of Questions from Both Testaments, found in St. Augustine, q. 114, says: "How praiseworthy the holy Tobias is, Scripture teaches us, whose devotion neither captivity diminished, nor the loss of his eyes persuaded him to cease blessing God; nor did the exhaustion of his substance turn him from the way of justice and truth. For necessity proves the just man. To preserve equity in poverty is true and perfect justice. Therefore, from that very source whence the devotion of some is diminished, Tobias, worthy of praise, makes increase." And a little later: "The mind of holy Tobias, raised toward God, broken neither by captivity nor humbled by want."

Thus the Saints gave thanks to God in blindness together with Tobias, and indeed loved blindness. St. Pigmenius the martyr was blind; when Julian the Apostate reproached him for his blindness, Pigmenius said: "I give eternal thanks to God most good and great that I do not see Julian." Finally, on account of his outstanding service rendered to the Christian faith and the destruction of the pagans' idol-monsters, he was thrown into the Tiber and from thence migrated to heaven; this one blind man saw more than all the sharp-eyed worshippers of phantoms. Didymus, the teacher of St. Jerome, was blind; Jerome himself calls him his "seeing one." Spes the Abbot was blind for fourteen years, and at his death his soul flew out in the form of a dove.
See more on this topic in our Matthew Rader.

Then Tobit sighed, and began to pray with tears. — He sighed deeply, for sighing is the voice of a soul oppressed by grief; and he poured forth his sorrow before God in prayer, which is the proper refuge of the afflicted.

Thou art just, O Lord, and all thy judgments are just. — He begins his prayer with praise of God's justice, acknowledging that whatever evils befall him are justly permitted by God on account of his sins. This is the mark of a truly holy soul: even in the midst of affliction to glorify God and confess His righteousness.

And now, O Lord, think of me, and take not revenge of my sins. — He asks God not to exact full punishment for his sins, but to deal with him in mercy. He acknowledges that the afflictions he suffers are the consequence of sin, both his own and that of his fathers.

For we have not obeyed thy commandments. — Here Tobit confesses the sins of his people collectively, recognizing that the Babylonian captivity and all the evils that followed were divine punishment for Israel's disobedience and idolatry.

And now, O Lord, great are thy judgments, because we have not done according to thy precepts. — God's judgments are "great," that is, severe and just, because they correspond to the greatness of the sins committed. Tobit here embraces God's chastisements as fully deserved.

And now deal with me according to thy will, and command my spirit to be received in peace. — Weary of life's sufferings and reproaches, Tobit asks for death, not from impatience or despair, but from a holy desire to be freed from sin and suffering and to rest in God. This is like the prayer of Elijah (1 Kings 19:4) and of Paul (Phil. 1:23). Note that he does not ask God to take his life in anger, but "in peace," which shows his trust in God's mercy. # GEM

On the same day it came to pass that Sara, daughter of Raguel, in Rages a city of the Medes, received a reproach from one of her father's servant maids. — God's providence is admirably shown here: on the very same day that Tobit prays for death, Sara also prays in distant Media. God hears both prayers together and provides one remedy for both. This simultaneity is not accidental but divinely ordered.

Because she had been given to seven husbands, and a devil named Asmodeus had killed them. — The name Asmodeus is Hebrew or Persian, meaning "the destroyer." This demon killed the seven husbands not out of love for Sara, as some fables suggest, but by God's permission, to exercise Sara's virtue and to provide occasion for the miraculous work of Raphael. The number seven signifies completeness of trial. # GEM

Dost thou not know that thou hast strangled thy husbands? — The maid cruelly taunts Sara, accusing her of being the cause of her husbands' deaths, perhaps through witchcraft or some hidden wickedness. This reproach, though unjust, was permitted by God to humble Sara and drive her to prayer.

When she heard these things she was very much grieved, insomuch that she thought of hanging herself. — Sara, overwhelmed by grief and shame, considered suicide. But she refrained, thinking of her father's grief. Here we see that even the holiest souls can be tempted to despair under extreme suffering. Yet the thought of filial duty restrained her — a lesson on how natural affections can serve as God's instrument to preserve life. # GEM

She therefore wept, and went up to her chamber, and for three days and three nights she neither ate nor drank. — The three days of fasting and weeping recall other great biblical fasts: Esther (Esth. 4:16), Paul after his conversion (Acts 9:9). Such prolonged fasting and prayer disposes the soul for special divine assistance.

Thou knowest, O Lord, that I never coveted a husband, and have kept my soul clean from all lust. — Sara protests her chastity before God. She had not married out of carnal desire but in obedience to the law requiring her to marry within her tribe. Her innocence before God is the foundation of her confident prayer. This is a model of how the pure in conscience can boldly approach God. # GEM

Never have I joined myself with them that play: neither have I made myself partaker with them that walk in lightness. — Sara declares that she has avoided all occasions of sin, all frivolous and immodest company. Chastity of soul requires not only avoiding sin itself but all the occasions and companions that lead to it.

But a husband I consented to take, with thy fear, not with my lust. — She married not for pleasure but out of reverence for God's law and the duty of continuing her family line. This is the proper motive for Christian marriage: not carnal desire but the honor of God and the continuation of a holy lineage.

And either I was unworthy of them, or they perhaps were not worthy of me: or thou hast perhaps reserved me for another man. — Sara humbly leaves the reason for her affliction to God's judgment, neither accusing her dead husbands nor condemning herself. This beautiful act of resignation to divine providence is a model for all who suffer inexplicable trials. # GEM

And now, O Lord, I set my eyes and my face toward thee. — As Tobit had prayed in Nineveh and Sara in Ecbatana, their prayers ascended together before God. This verse introduces the divine response: the sending of Raphael. Note the theological point: God hears simultaneous prayers from distant places, for He is omnipresent and omniscient.

And Raphael was sent to heal them both. — The name Raphael means "God heals" or "the medicine of God." He was sent to heal Tobit's blindness and to free Sara from the demon. The Fathers see in this a figure of Christ, the true Raphael, who heals the blindness of the soul and delivers it from the power of the devil. # GEM

Tobias broke forth into a hymn of praise, giving thanks to God for his own blindness and the captivity of his people, because through these afflictions God had brought him and his people to repentance and salvation. This is the mark of a holy soul, to praise God not only in prosperity but also in adversity, recognizing that chastisements come from a Father's love.

He hath chastised us for our iniquities, and he will save us for his own mercy. God's justice and mercy are here beautifully joined: justice in the chastisement, mercy in the salvation. #GEM Augustine says: "God is good when he gives, and good when he takes away; good in prosperity, good in adversity."

Confess ye to him before the Gentiles. Tobias exhorts the captive Israelites to give public praise to God even among the pagans, so that by their example and testimony the Gentiles themselves might be moved to the knowledge and worship of the true God.

God is called "our Father" and "our God forever and ever," signifying the eternal covenant between God and Israel, a covenant not dissolved by captivity but rather purified by it. The fatherly care of God for his people endures even through their punishment.

He will gather us from all nations. This is a prophecy of the return from Babylonian captivity, and mystically of the final gathering of the elect from the four corners of the earth at the Last Day. The dispersion of Israel was not final destruction but a medicinal scattering, followed by a merciful regathering.

If you turn to him with all your heart and with all your soul. The condition of restoration is sincere conversion. Half-hearted repentance does not suffice; God demands the whole heart. This is the constant teaching of Scripture and the Fathers. #GEM Bernard says: "God is not mocked; he sees the heart and rewards according to its sincerity."

I will confess to him in the land of my captivity. Tobias sets an example of praising God not only in Jerusalem and the Temple, but even in a foreign land, among idolaters. True worship is not bound to a place but to the disposition of the soul. This prefigures Christian worship in spirit and in truth.

Let all men speak, and let them give him thanks in Jerusalem. Tobias desires that the praise of God resound universally, from all peoples and in the holy city. This points prophetically to the universal Church, the new Jerusalem, in which all nations shall praise God through Christ.

Jerusalem is addressed directly and exhorted to bless God. The city itself, as the dwelling place of God and center of worship, is called to give thanks. The Fathers see here a figure of the Church, which is the spiritual Jerusalem, the mother of all the faithful.

Because he will bring them again to thee. The return of the exiles to Jerusalem is promised. God does not abandon his city forever. This is both a literal historical prophecy fulfilled under Cyrus, and a mystical prophecy of the restoration of souls to grace and of the blessed to the heavenly Jerusalem.

And the light of his majesty shall be upon thee. The glory of God shall dwell again in Jerusalem. This was fulfilled imperfectly in the Second Temple, and perfectly in the Incarnation, when the Word of God, the true Light, tabernacled among men in the city of Jerusalem. #GEM Cyril of Alexandria says this light is Christ himself, the Sun of Justice.

Cursed are all they that hate thee, and blessed shall all be that love thee. This solemn blessing and curse mirrors the blessings and curses of the Law. Those who love the Church of God, which Jerusalem figures, shall be blessed; those who persecute or hate her shall be cursed. The enemies of the Church are enemies of God himself.

Rejoice and be glad for the children of the just. The joy of Jerusalem is not selfish but communal; she rejoices in the salvation of her children. The just who have suffered in captivity shall see their children gathered and blessed. This consolation is offered to all who endure present suffering with patience.

Blessed are all they that love thee, and that rejoice in thy peace. Peace is the great gift promised to the new Jerusalem. #GEM Augustine in the City of God says: "The peace of the heavenly city is the perfectly ordered and harmonious enjoyment of God, and of one another in God." This verse points to the beatitude of the saints in the eternal Jerusalem.

My soul, bless the Lord, the great King. Tobias turns inward and exhorts his own soul to praise God. The praise of God must begin within, in the soul's own depths, before it can be expressed outwardly. The title "great King" recalls Psalm 47, where Jerusalem is called the city of the great King.

For Jerusalem shall be built up with sapphires and emeralds. The description of the new Jerusalem adorned with precious stones is prophetic and mystical. The literal meaning refers to the restoration and beautification of the earthly city; the spiritual meaning, followed by nearly all the Fathers, refers to the heavenly Jerusalem adorned with the virtues and merits of the saints. #GEM The Venerable Bede expounds each precious stone as a distinct virtue of the elect.

And her streets shall be paved with white and clean stone. The whiteness and purity of the stones signifies the holiness of the citizens of the heavenly Jerusalem. Nothing impure shall enter there. The streets being paved signifies the ordered and beautiful life of the blessed, walking in the way of God without obstacle.

Alleluia shall be sung in her streets. This Hebrew word of praise, meaning "Praise ye the Lord," shall resound eternally in the heavenly city. #GEM Augustine says: "There shall be the leisure of Alleluia, for we shall see God; Alleluia shall be our occupation, for we shall praise God forever." The book fittingly closes this canticle with the word that shall never cease in eternity. Those who are called fabricators of lies and worshippers of false idols are excluded from this city; only the lovers of truth and the true God shall enter and sing the eternal Alleluia.
who endures temptation; because, when he has been proved, he shall receive the crown of life. Blessed was Job,

Where is your hope? — as if to say: Your hope has deceived you and was false and deceptive; for you hoped through almsgiving to obtain all prosperity from God, and behold God through the burial of the dead has blinded you and deprived you of your eyes. For what is more unhappy and miserable than one robbed of sight and made blind?

We are children of holy men — namely of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, etc.; let us therefore imitate their faith, hope, patience, and constancy in adversity. Let the afflicted Christian say the same: I am a son of Christ; therefore I will continually look upon His passion and cross, and imitate His patience. I am a son of the holy Apostles and Martyrs, such as Saint Paul, Saint Lawrence, Saint Stephen, Saint Vincent, etc., who endured so many and such great things for Christ: therefore I will follow them, and will bear even my small evil willingly and bravely for the love of Christ. For I am a son of the Saints, and therefore I myself am holy, that is, faithful, dedicated to God, and called by Him to perfect holiness, which consists in patience. For patience has a perfect work, James 1:4.

And we await that life which God will give to those who never change their faith toward Him. — Tobias responds fittingly to those who mock him: "Where is your hope?" — as if to say: My hope is not directed toward present goods, but future ones. For I hope for another blessed and eternal life after this one, which God will give to those who steadfastly persist in faith and hope in Him through whatever adversities. This hope of another and happy life inspired the seven Maccabean brothers to overcome, indeed to despise, frying pans, drums, the rack, and all torments; whence they said fearlessly to the tyrant Antiochus: "You, most wicked one, destroy us in the present life, but the King of the world will raise us up, who die for His laws, in the resurrection of eternal life," 2 Macc. 7:9. The same was felt and said by nearly all the other martyrs without exception, who, as the Apostle says in Heb. 11:37, "were stoned, were sawn asunder, were tempted, were slain with the sword," etc., "not accepting deliverance, that they might obtain a better resurrection"; because also Christ...

For the joy set before him (the joy of the imminent resurrection and glorious ascension), he endured the cross, despising the shame. St. Francis imitated him, saying: "So great is the glory I await, that every pain delights me."

WHENCE IT CAME TO PASS THAT WHEN HE HAD BROUGHT HOME THE KID OF THE GOATS] that is, into the house. The Greek adds that this kid was given to Anna as a bonus above the wages of her weaving; and since Tobias plainly did not believe Anna when she said this, she, angry, mocked him saying: "Your hope has come to nothing," etc. Tobias had occasion to doubt whether it was stolen, both because such a kid had never appeared in his house, and such a thing is not usually given as wages to weavers, nor had he ever received such, yet Anna had brought it; and also because Anna was poor — he feared therefore lest poverty had compelled her to bring in a straying and lost kid into the house; and furthermore because he himself was a most fervent lover of justice and keenest hater of theft, and likewise of most tender and delicate conscience. # GEM "For it is the mark of good minds to fear fault where there is no fault," says St. Bernard. In like manner, St. Sanctulus, as recorded by St. Gregory in Book 3 of the Dialogues, in the utmost want of provisions and famine, fearing lest bread divinely offered might perhaps belong to another, and lest out of piety he might commit a fault, was afraid to set it before his hired servants.

The demon, he says, is called from δαιμόνιον [daimonion], that is, to terrify, because it terrifies men. For as St. Anthony says in St. Athanasius: if demons find in the hearts of evil minds a seed of fear and dread, like robbers who hold deserted places, they heap up accumulated fears, and cruelly pressing down, they punish the unhappy soul. St. Athanasius adds: A young man was brought to St. Anthony who had a principal demon; sending him away to his disciple he said: This is not my work. For against this demon of the highest order, that is the principal one, I have not yet been granted grace; but this is the grace of Paul the Simple, as Palladius narrates in ch. 28. # GEM

Fourth, Eugubinus in book 8 of de perenni philosophia, ch. 19, derives the Greek δαίμων from the Hebrew סְדִים sedim, or by crasis, sdaim, that is, destroyers, devastators; for such they are, and such especially is Asmodaeus.

You ask who Asmodaeus is. The Rabbis in Elias in the Thisbi assert first, that Asmodaeus was born of Naamah, who was the sister of Tubalcain and wife of Shimron, Gen. 4 v. 22. For there are four mothers of demons, namely Naamah, or as they name her, Nahemar, Lilith, Ogereth, and Machlath. Second, that Asmodaeus is he who in the Chaldaic paraphrase of Job 28 is called Sammael, who tempted and overcame Eve, and harasses married couples, and is the author of all quarrels. With him Sara had her contest, who aided by an angel of God went away victorious: whence she herself received her name from conquering and prevailing. For Sara in Hebrew is the same as ruling and lady. This latter is fitting and true.

Third, Eccles. 1.1, I the Preacher was King; the Chaldean translates: Aschmadai malca deschedim, that is, Asmodaeus king of demons. The Zohar adds that Solomon, expelled from his kingdom by Asmodaeus, was detained in the desert; and Asmodaeus himself meanwhile assumed the likeness of Solomon and administered justice to the Hebrews in Jerusalem. But these are the inventions of the Rabbis, and therefore figments.

I say therefore that Asmodaeus is the demon who drives men to lust, and punishes and badly maltreats those overcome by him, as he badly maltreated the seven husbands of Sara on account of their impurity. That this is so is gathered from the power granted to Asmodaeus in ch. 6 v. 17: For those who receive marriage in such a way as to shut out God from themselves and from their mind, and to give themselves over to their lust like the horse and the mule which have no understanding, over them the demon has power. Now demons tend to be the torturers of those very ones whom they have deceived. For by whom a man is overcome, of this man he is also the slave, 2 Pet. 2:19. Against those therefore, as against the guilty and his own chattels, Asmodaeus rages with his torments.

Hence Asmodaeus, or a companion or subordinate of Asmodaeus, was that demon whom Cyprian, first a magician but afterward a believer and martyr, summoned by his incantations to bend St. Justina, virgin and martyr, toward love of the young man who was courting her — but in vain. For St. Justina overcame him by the sign of the cross, prayer, and constancy. Such also was that demon who, possessing a girl enchanted by a young magician, when asked by St. Hilarion why he had not rather possessed the young magician himself, answered: Why should I enter into him who had my colleague the demon of love? as St. Jerome relates in the life of St. Hilarion.

Finally, hear St. Athanasius in the life of St. Anthony. That demon, throwing himself at the feet of Anthony, wept in a human voice, saying: You have brought back many whom I had deceived; but now, as by the other Saints, so also by your labor I am overcome. When Anthony asked who it was that said such things, he replied: I am the friend of fornication, I have taken up manifold weapons of shamelessness against all young men, and hence I am called the spirit of fornication. How many planning to live chastely have I deceived! how many beginning with small lapses have I persuaded to return to their former filth! I am he on account of whom the prophet rebukes the fallen saying: You have been seduced by the spirit of fornication; and truly by me they had been overthrown. I am he who often tempted you yourself, and was always repulsed. # GEM

You ask secondly why he is called Asmodaeus. Serarius replies first: Asmodaeus is called as if from אֵשׁ es, that is, fire, of Meda, that is, of Media, because he was king of all the demons who then inhabited Media (in which was Rages, where Sara lived, and her seven husbands were killed by Asmodaeus) and the neighboring provinces. For since he was active in Media, where else should a king be but in that very region? He therefore stirred up all the Medes to lust, and afflicted those he had overcome.

For there are Persian demons, Median, African, Southern, Meridional, sepulchral, aerial, fiery, aquatic, and earthly, as Rhodiginus teaches in book 2 ch. 4 and 5, Chaldean Cant. 4:6, and Thomas of Cantimpré, book 1.2 de Apibus ch. 57 n. 9.

Second, Asmodaeus, or as it is said in Hebrew Asmodai, signifies an abundance of sin; for אָשָׁם ascam is sin, ד dai is abundance; for where lust burns, there sins abound. For as St. Gregory says on 1 Kings ch. 15: After the manner of fire lust is kindled, and if it is not extinguished with care, the neighboring stubble is quickly set aflame.

Third, Asmodaeus is the same as אֵשׁ es, that is fire, or אָשָׁם ascam, that is sin, מוֹדֵד moded, that is measuring and measuring out, so as to explore how great the concupiscence is, and to inflict a punishment commensurate with the fault.
Fourth, Asmodaeus is the same as destroyer, desolator, from אָשָׁם ascam, that is he desolated, or from שָׁמַד scamad, that is he destroyed.

Fifth and most aptly, Asmodaeus is called as if from אֵשׁ es, that is fire, or אָשָׁם ascam, that is of sin, שׁוֹמֵד scomed, that is the destroyer. For he is presented here as the avenger of lust and the killer of unchaste bridegrooms.

AS SOON AS THEY HAD GONE IN TO HER. That is, before they were with her as with wives, that is before they came together. For God did not permit Sara, so chaste, to be touched by such unchaste men, but killed them through Asmodaeus before any touch, because they wished to assault her with unbridled lust.

HENCEFORTH MAY WE SEE NO SON OR DAUGHTER OF YOURS ON THE EARTH, O MURDERESS OF YOUR HUSBANDS. This is the serious insult hurled by the maidservant, or as in the Greek, by the maidservants, against Sara. The Greek makes it worse; for it reads: And they said to her: Do you not understand, you who strangle your husbands? Behold you have had seven, and have not been named for even one of them. Why do you beat us? If they are dead, go with them: may we never see your son or daughter in this age.

Note here three insults cast at Sara by the maidservant: first, that she had killed, indeed strangled, her seven husbands, when she had not done this herself but Asmodaeus had. For what is more shameful, what more atrocious to cast at a bride, than to say: You killed your bridegroom?

Second, that she perverts her name; for instead of Sara, that is "lady," she calls her in Hebrew *tsaram*, that is, "afflicted and wretched." Third, she imprecates upon her perpetual sterility. Thus St. Monica, called a wine-bibber by a maidservant, was greatly afflicted, as St. Augustine relates in Confessions, Book 9, ch. 8. So also Sara here was so tormented that in v. 15 she prays: "I beseech thee, O Lord, that thou wouldst either deliver me from this reproach, or else take me away from the earth."

She went up to an upper chamber. # GEM In Greek it reads: she was moved, *hōste apagchasthai*, which Vatablus translates: "so that she thought to strangle herself"; but checking herself and recovering, she said: "I am the only child of my father; if I do this, it will be an everlasting disgrace to him, and a most ready occasion of death, for I would bring his old age with sorrow to the grave," that is, to death or the sepulchre, as in ch. 6 v. 7. So also the Hebrew has: "She was so afflicted that she desired to strangle herself; and she would have done so, had she not thought again: so shall I bring the grey hairs of my father and my mother with sorrow to the sepulchre." Better is the rendering of the interpreter of the Greek Royal Bibles: "She was saddened so as to be suffocated," namely, from the anguish of grief and sorrow.

AND FOR THREE DAYS AND THREE NIGHTS SHE DID NOT EAT OR DRINK, BUT CONTINUED IN PRAYER. See here the fruit of contemptuous insult; for it produced compunction, and this in turn produced a three-day fast and prayer. For, as Lyranus says: The fervour of prayer and its prolonged duration induces forgetfulness of food and drink. And the Gloss says: He is nourished by prayer, whom the leanness of the body feeds.

Blessed be thy name, O God. The Greek texts have more fully: "Blessed art thou, O Lord my God, and blessed is thy holy and glorious name forever. Let all thy works praise thee forever." By this three-day prayer Sara obtained from God resignation and tranquillity of soul together with great confidence in God, so that beginning a new prayer she burst forth into the praise of God.

Here begins the account of how Tobias, returning from the feast of Pentecost with joy, learned that a dead man lay unburied in the street; he immediately left his meal and went to bury him, at risk to himself, since King Sennacherib had forbidden such burials and had already sought to kill Tobias for doing this very thing.

Tobias, at the feast of Pentecost, having prepared a good dinner, said to his son: Go and bring whomsoever thou shalt find of our brethren who are mindful of God. This shows his piety: he would not feast without inviting the poor and devout brethren to share his table.

The young man, returning, told him: Father, one of our people lieth slain in the street. Tobias was immediately moved with compassion, leaving his meal untouched, thus showing that works of mercy toward the dead take precedence even over bodily refreshment.

He arose before he had tasted his meat, and took the body from the street and carried it privately to his house, that after the sun was set he might bury him. Note that he hid the body, for fear of the king's prohibition, acting prudently while still fulfilling the work of mercy.

He hid the body until nightfall, then buried it, fearing God more than the king. # GEM Ambrose, De Officiis: "Tobias, though surrounded by captives bereft of all things, yet buried the dead, which many strong and rich men dare not do."

And Tobias remembered the word of the prophet Amos: Your feasts shall be turned into mourning and all your mirth into lamentation. Here sacred Scripture is cited to show that afflictions rightly follow upon sin, and that the devout soul draws consolation from the word of God even in grief.

After the sun was set he went and buried him. He buried him secretly by night, at personal risk, showing that the duty of burying the dead is not annulled by human prohibition when God's law commands it.

His neighbors came and rebuked him, saying: Already thou wast commanded to be slain for this matter, and thou hast again buried the dead. The wicked mock the pious for their constancy in virtue; but the just man is not turned aside by human threats.

But Tobias fearing God more than the king, carried off the bodies of them that were slain, and hid them in his house, and at midnight buried them. Here is a pattern of fortitude joined to piety: he feared God above all earthly powers.

Now it happened one day that being wearied with burying, he came to his house and cast himself down by the wall and slept. And while he slept, hot dung out of a swallow's nest fell upon his eyes and he became blind. God permitted this affliction to befall His servant, not as punishment for sin, but for the greater manifestation of His glory and the proving of Tobias's virtue. # GEM Gregory the Great, Moralia: "The saints are sometimes afflicted in body precisely when they are most fervent in good works, that they may not glory in their own virtue but in God alone."

This trial God permitted for the increase of his merit, as the angel Raphael later declares: Because thou wast acceptable to God, it was necessary that temptation should prove thee. Thus afflictions are not signs of divine wrath toward the just, but of divine love and fatherly correction.
His wife Anna worked for hire at weaving. Note the poverty and humility of Tobias: once wealthy, now reduced to blindness and want, yet he murmured not against God.
She received a young kid and brought it home. Some suspected she had stolen it or received it improperly, which gave occasion for the bitter exchange that follows.

When Tobias heard it bleating, he said: Take heed lest it be stolen; restore it to its owners, for it is not lawful for us to eat anything that is stolen. Here Tobias shows his scrupulous justice and integrity, even in extreme poverty; he would not profit by anything of doubtful ownership.

His wife answered in anger: It is evident thy hope is come to nothing, and thy alms are forgotten. This reproach, though bitter and unjust, gave Tobias occasion for the beautiful prayer that follows, in which he confesses his sins, extols God's justice, and implores divine mercy. # GEM Ambrose, Inst. Virg.: "A good prayer is one that observes order, that we begin first with divine praises. For if, when we deal with a man, we wish to make the judge favorable to us, how much more when we entreat our Lord? First therefore let us offer to God the sacrifice of humiliation of our sins, and then our petition."

and he will remit sins in the day of tribulation. Again — Lapide: almsgiving obtains remission of sins in the day of tribulation; this is a foundational principle underlying Tobias's practice of mercy throughout this chapter.

Thou knowest, O Lord, that I never coveted a man — that is, I did not seek him out of lust and carnal desire, but received him only for the sake of offspring, to obey my parents and to provide them with grandchildren and heirs.

I kept my soul clean from all concupiscence — not only of lust, but also of gluttony, pride, avarice, and all other enticements. Great was this purity and continence of Sara, on account of which she merited to be heard by God and to be blessed.

Never have I joined myself with those who play — First, the Rabbis take this as referring to immodest play, such as lewd fornicators engage in. In this way they explain Exodus 32:6: "The people sat down to eat and drink, and rose up to play," that is, to fornicate.

Better is the interpretation of Hugo and others, who take "play" to mean any play familiar to young girls, especially dancing and the choral dance. Hugo says that a wise and upright man, following Caesarius (bk. 4, c. 11), calls dancing "the devil's play." And Matthew Glingius, in his Commonplaces, in the chapter on dancing and the choral dance, says: "What is the choral dance? It is a circle whose center is the devil and whose circumference are all his angels." # GEM For this reason the Blessed Virgin, as witnesses testify...

Tunc vocavit Tobias filium suum, etc. — Then Tobias called his son, etc. Tobias the elder, now recovered from blindness, called his son Tobias the younger, and deliberated with him about what reward should be given to the angel Raphael, who had accompanied him on the journey and restored his sight.

Note here the gratitude and liberality of Tobias toward his companion and guide. This is a model for us: we ought to be grateful and generous to those who have helped us, especially in spiritual matters.

Dixit autem ei: Quid dabimus viro isti sancto? — And he said to him: What shall we give to this holy man? Tobias calls Raphael a holy man, because he appeared in the form of a man and conducted himself with all holiness and virtue throughout the journey.

Pater, quam mercedem dabimus ei? — Father, what reward shall we give him? The younger Tobias recounts the benefits received: that Raphael led him, protected him, healed him, recovered the money, and obtained Sara as his wife.

Et dixit Tobias: Dignum est, fili, ut ei dimidiam partem omnium, quae allata sunt, tribuamus. — And Tobias said: It is fitting, son, that we give him half of all that has been brought. This is a remarkable example of generosity: to offer half of one's entire fortune as a reward. Tobias did not bargain or calculate meanly, but gave most liberally.

Et convocans eum pater et filius — And calling him, the father and son. Both Tobias the father and Tobias the son called Raphael together, showing their united consent and gratitude. They offered him half of all their goods as his due reward.

Tunc secreto allocutus eos dixit: Benedicite Deum caeli — Then he spoke to them privately and said: Bless the God of heaven. Raphael, before revealing himself, first turns their attention to God. He exhorts them to bless and praise God above all things, so that all gratitude might be directed first to God, who is the author of all good things, and from whom all benefits ultimately flow.

# GEM Here Raphael teaches the fundamental lesson of all piety: that God must be blessed and praised before all else. Augustine says that every benefit received from creatures ought to lead us back to the praise of God the Creator. The creature is only an instrument; God is the principal cause of all goods.

Et coram omnibus viventibus confitemini ei — And confess him before all the living. Raphael urges a public and open confession and praise of God, not merely a private and hidden thanksgiving. We owe God not only interior gratitude but exterior and public confession of His benefits.

Quia fecit vobiscum misericordiam suam — Because he has shown his mercy to you. The reason for praising God is His mercy, that is, the benefits He has bestowed on Tobias and his family. All temporal and spiritual blessings are fruits of divine mercy.

Sacramentum regis abscondere bonum est — It is good to hide the secret of a king. This is a proverbial saying: just as it is right to keep secret the counsels and secrets of an earthly king, so much more ought the secret works of God to be received with reverent silence and awe. Raphael here prepares Tobias and his son for the revelation of his angelic nature.

Opera autem Dei revelare et confiteri honorificum est — But it is honorable to reveal and confess the works of God. There is a distinction: the secrets of kings are to be kept, but the works of God, His miracles and benefits, are to be declared and published openly, for His glory and the edification of others.

Bona est oratio cum jejunio et eleemosyna — Prayer is good with fasting and almsgiving. Raphael commends three works of piety: prayer, fasting, and almsgiving. These three are the chief exercises of the Christian and Jewish religious life, as also our Lord confirms in Matthew 6 when He speaks of almsgiving, prayer, and fasting.

# GEM St. Thomas Aquinas (II-II, q. 83) teaches that prayer, fasting, and almsgiving are the three principal remedies against the three chief sins: almsgiving against avarice, fasting against lust and gluttony, prayer against pride and spiritual negligence. Raphael here anticipates the full Christian doctrine of penance and satisfaction.

Quam thesaurizare aurum — Rather than to lay up gold. Almsgiving and works of piety are more valuable than worldly riches. This is the teaching of the whole of Scripture: treasure in heaven is preferable to treasure on earth. Cf. Matthew 6:20.

Eleemosyna enim a morte liberat — For almsgiving delivers from death. This is one of the most celebrated texts in the book of Tobit. Almsgiving liberates from eternal death, from the death of the soul, and also frequently from temporal death. This is confirmed by Proverbs 10:2 and 11:4: "Almsgiving delivers from death."

# GEM St. Cyprian, in his treatise De opere et eleemosynis, comments extensively on this verse, saying: "Almsgiving is a great comfort for sins, a great remedy for the soul's wounds; it extinguishes the fire of sins, and by the merit of mercy it earns the mercy of God." This is a gem of patristic teaching on the saving power of almsgiving.

Et ipsa est quae purgat peccata — And it is that which purges sins. Almsgiving has the power to purge sins, that is, to obtain forgiveness of sins, not by its own intrinsic merit, but insofar as it is an act of charity and repentance, and insofar as God in His mercy accepts it as satisfaction for sin. See Daniel 4:24.

Et facit invenire misericordiam et vitam aeternam — And makes one find mercy and eternal life. The ultimate fruit of almsgiving is eternal life, because it disposes the soul to receive God's mercy and final salvation. This is not a Pelagian teaching but affirms that works of charity, done in the state of grace, merit eternal life by God's gracious ordination.

Qui autem delinquunt et peccant, hostes sunt animae suae — But they who commit sin and iniquity are enemies of their own soul. Contrariwise, those who sin and do not repent, who hoard their goods without mercy to the poor, are enemies of their own soul, because they bring upon themselves eternal death. Sin is ultimately self-destructive.

Manifestabo itaque vobis veritatem — Therefore I will declare the truth to you. Now Raphael prepares to reveal his true identity. He says he will tell them the full truth about himself, which he had concealed for a providential purpose. The time of concealment is now past, and the truth must be made known for the glory of God.

Quando orabas cum lacrymis — When thou didst pray with tears. Raphael reveals that he was present before God, presenting the prayers of Tobias. This is a key text for the doctrine of angelic intercession: angels present the prayers of the saints before God. Cf. Revelation 8:3-4, where an angel offers incense with the prayers of the saints.

# GEM The Council of Trent and the Fathers, especially St. Basil and St. Jerome, cite this text to confirm that angels are intercessors and mediators of our prayers before God. Lapide notes that this does not detract from Christ's unique mediation, since angels intercede in and through Christ, as subordinate ministers of the one Mediator.

Et quia sepelibas mortuos — And because thou didst bury the dead. Among the works of mercy which Raphael declares were pleasing to God and which he presented before the divine throne, the burial of the dead is given special prominence. This confirms the traditional teaching that burial of the dead is a corporal work of mercy pleasing to God.

Et quia non es tardus surrexisti — And because thou wast not slow to rise up. Tobias showed alacrity and promptness in works of mercy: he did not hesitate or delay, but acted immediately when he knew someone needed help. Promptness and readiness in virtue is itself meritorious and pleasing to God.

Ego enim sum Raphael angelus — For I am Raphael the angel. Here Raphael reveals his identity: he is one of the seven angels who stand before God. This is a solemn and dramatic revelation. The name Raphael means "God heals" or "medicine of God," which was entirely fitting for his mission of healing Tobias and his family.

Unus ex septem qui adstamus ante Dominum — One of seven who stand before the Lord. Scripture here confirms the existence of seven angels of special dignity who stand perpetually before God in His immediate presence. These seven are the highest of the angels, the archangels or princes of the angelic host. They are mentioned also in Revelation 8:2: "the seven angels who stand before God."

# GEM St. Gregory the Great (Homily 34 on the Gospels) identifies these seven angels as the chief princes of the angelic hierarchy, saying: "There are seven spirits before the throne of God, because the fullness of the Spirit is signified by the number seven." The names of the seven given in Jewish tradition are: Michael, Gabriel, Raphael, Uriel, Saraqael, Raguel, and Remiel.

Et cum essetis vobiscum — And when I was with you. Raphael clarifies that when he appeared to be eating and drinking with them, he was not truly eating and drinking as a man, but only in appearance. This confirms the patristic teaching that angels can assume bodies and appear to eat, but do not truly consume food as men do.

Voluntate Dei eram — I was by the will of God. Everything Raphael did was by the express will and command of God. He was not acting on his own initiative but as the messenger and minister of divine providence. This shows the perfect obedience of angels to God's will.

Et benedicite illum — And bless him. After revealing himself, Raphael commands them again to bless and praise God. The first and last word of the angel is: give all glory to God. This is the model of all true ministry: to deflect all praise from oneself to God alone.

Visum est mihi tentare vos — It seemed good to me to try you. Raphael says he came to try or test them, that is, to prove and exercise their virtue, their faith, their patience, and their generosity. God permits His servants to be tested so that their virtue may be made manifest and rewarded.

Nolite timere — Fear not. The angel reassures Tobias and his son, who had fallen prostrate in fear at the revelation of his angelic nature. This formula, "Fear not," is the classical angelic greeting throughout Scripture, from the appearances in Genesis to those in the Gospels and Revelation.
